terms.title: Albedo
terms.summary: Base color map without lighting, shadow, or highlights.

terms.title: SubD
terms.summary:
SubD is short for "Subdivision", a modeling technique in 3D modeling that transforms a low-poly model into a high-poly model by dividing its surface into smaller, smoother polygons. 

This modeling technique is for making high-resolution models, by manipulating a lower-resolution "cage" model and using software to subdivide for a smoother surface. While common in video game art for "Hard Surface" assets, SubD is paramount in Film/"off-line" rendering, as it provides "infinite detail", as no matter how close you zoom in to a curve for any particular film shot, you can always just divide the cage another time to make it smoother for that shot. While overkill for many game assets, it saves a ton of potential lost work in the event one needs to "get closer" to an asset than originally planned.

Subdividing increases the number of vertices on the model, making curves more rounded. Usually each subdivision level increases the vertex count 4x the original count.

For game artists, "high-poly hard-surface" usually means creating mechanical/constructed models, which are used to bake into Normal Maps and other types of textures, which are in turn used on lower-resolution game-friendly models.

Subdivision surfaces can also be used for organic shapes.

Not to be confused with SmoothingGroups. Sometimes the term "box modeling" is used, though this applies only to the cage modeling process, not to the subdivision.

terms.title: Baking
terms.summary: Baking is the act of transferring data from one asset/form to another. In 3D Art, it often refers to baking the surface normals of a high poly mesh into a texture (normal map) that a low poly can use, but you can also bake: BaseColor, Curvature, AO, Thickness, ID maps, etc.). For animation, it often refers to baking animation data for every key, so you can have a "Rig" controller key framed (or driven by noise) that then gets "baked" onto the joints/bones of the skeleton for use in bake or to transfer between apps.

terms.title: Wireframe
terms.summary: The wire-frame method of modelling consists of only lines and curves that connect the points or vertices and thereby define the edges of an object.

terms.title: nGon
terms.summary: An Ngon (or N-gon) is a polygon with 5 or more edges. Anything that is not a tri or quad is considered an Ngon.

terms.title: Pole
terms.summary: A pole is a set of edges that merge into a single vertex. Avoiding poles with six or more edges on a curved surfaces is something that you should incorporate into your modeling workflow.

terms.title: High Poly
terms.summary: A mesh that is high in polygonal density (Lots of faces/verts/edges), and contains all the surface detail you want visually. This can be either a "Subdivided" or "Smooth Mesh Preview" of the "Base Mesh" in Maya/Blender/etc from the "SubD" workflow, or a sculpted mesh from ZBrush/Blender, or a high quality output from CAD software (Plasticity/Fusion 360), or even just boolean mesh that has lots of topology to ensure it looks nice/smooth/round where expected.

terms.title: Topology
terms.summary:
Topology in 3D modeling is the study and application of how surfaces are constructed from vertices, edges, and faces. It's the blueprint of your model, defining its structure and ensuring that it can be manipulated without causing unwanted distortion or deformation. 
- For Game Art "Good Topology" means a wireframe that supports silhouette, shading, UVs, Materials and nothing more, so edge loops should terminate intro triangles if they aren't required.
- For SubD, "Good Topology" means evenly spaced edge loops for curved surfaces, and mitigating "poles" (vertices that connect with either more or less than 4 edges connecting). Idea Geometry would be as simple of a mesh as possible that subdivides to the correct shape, likely resulting in few if any tris & nGons. Creasing and/or triple edging is used to control edges/curvature.
- For Digital Sculpting, "Good Topology" would be ALL quads (to ensure you can preserve your base mesh reconstruction) and *EVENLY SPACED* "Edge Loops", so that you have consistent sculpting resolution (the polygon sculpting equivalent to texture painting's "Texel Scale". Maybe dub this, "Polygrid Sculpt Scale"? ;)

terms.title: Normal Map
terms.summary: Texture that encodes surface directions to fake small-scale detail.

terms.title: Low Poly
terms.summary:
aka "Render Mesh", "Arbitrary Mesh", "Game Res Mesh", "Mid-Poly" (a game res mesh that usually has more polys than a "Low Poly" because it uses trim sheets and tiling/world textures instead of the High Poly/Low Poly "Bake" workflow). 

A mesh that is "Low" in polygonal density (no unnecessary faces/verts/edges). This is the mesh you actually use "In-Game" and it's topology is decided not by edge flow or "SubD rules" but instead solely by the needs got eh video game asset (edges/verts/polys to support silhouette, shading, UVs, other game data). This mesh more than likely would consist of triangles to ensure it's efficient. 99% of the time when done well, this mesh is NOT the same thing as the mesh you subdivide to make your SubD High Poly ("Base Cage", Base Mesh", i.e. Lowest Subdivision in ZBrush or non-"smooth mesh preview" mesh in Maya).

terms.title: UV Unwrapping
terms.summary: Flattening a 3D model's surface into a 2D layout for texturing.

terms.title: Rigging
terms.summary: Creating bones and controllers so a model can be posed and animated.

terms.title: PBR Workflow
terms.summary: Physically Based Rendering approach using energy-conserving materials.

terms.title: Topology
terms.summary: Arrangement and flow of polygons that affect deformation and shading.

terms.title: IK vs. FK
terms.summary: Two approaches for controlling limbs in character animation.

terms.title: HDRI
terms.summary: High dynamic range images used to light scenes with realistic reflections.

terms.title: Ambient Occlusion
terms.summary: Shading that darkens creases and corners where light is occluded.

terms.title: Keyframe
terms.summary: A defined value at a specific time that drives animation curves.
